EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of increasing wind aloft and a tightening surface pressure gradient generated strong wind around and west of Clark, and along the U.S. Highway 287 corridor from near Muddy Gap to Beaver Rim. Peak wind gusts of 77 to 88 mph were recorded in and near the mouth of the Clark's Fork River Canyon. Nearby, estimated peak wind gusts of 60 mph occurred just west of Clark. Sustained southwest wind of 40 to 45 mph was recorded around 0700MST by an automated sensor on Beaver Rim. Strong wind gusts increased again during the late evening at Jeffrey City, where a peak gust to 59 mph was measured at 2105MST.

A strong low level pressure gradient over the Rocky Mountains was expected to produce strong winds over portions of southeast Wyoming. The strong winds were expected to produce low visibilities in areas of blowing snow due to recent snowfall in addition to dangerous wind chill values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds varied through much of December 9th, with periods of high winds followed by periods of decreased winds. Three distinct periods of high winds were measured by Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor at Vedauwoo (KVDW). The sensor measured sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH on from 422 AM through 1227 PM, with a peak gust of 69 MPH around 1015 AM LST. Additionally, winds in excess of 40 MPH were measured from 327 PM through 522 PM and 9/1002 PM through 10/0202 AM LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large surface pressure gradient over the Rocky Mountains produced a short-lived, but strong wind event over portions of southeast Wyoming in the afternoon of December 12.  The winds near the Interstate 25 Bordeaux exit knocked over a semi-truck around 1300 LST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor near the Bordeaux exit of Interstate 25 measured a peak gust of 75 MPH between 1300 and 1315 LST with sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH from 1150 to 1415 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent northwest flow aloft ushered a series of weather disturbances across Wyoming bringing heavy snow to the mountains of northern Wyoming. Mountain snowfall of 12 to 18 inches was common. A prolonged period of light snow allowed accumulations to top-out between six and eight inches in Jackson Hole and the Star Valley.

Strong mountain top wind from the west-northwest produced sustained wind of 89 mph over Chief Joseph Pass along State Highway 296. The sustained speed over the pass was almost continuously above 60 mph for about seven hours. Southwest of Cody, a gust to 65 mph was measured about six miles up the valley of the South Fork of the Shoshone River.

Strong northwest flow along the crest of the Absaroka Range descended the lee slope producing sustained wind of 79 mph along State Highway 296 at Chief Joseph Pass. The wind was sustained at 56 mph or more for the better part of two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, moist westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean provided a prolonged period of snowfall across northwest and far west Wyoming. Mountain snowfall generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches. The highest amounts were mainly confined to a swath from northeast Teton County, across southern Yellowstone National Park, and into the central Absaroka Range. For the lower elevations, the heaviest snow totals of four to eight inches occurred in the Bondurant Valley.

Blizzard conditions occurred over some areas with many roads closed due to blowing and drifting snow and extremmely low visibilities along with numerous accidents.

A severe thunderstorm traveled along Interstate 80 for about 40 miles through eastern Sweetwater County. The storm produced large hail, damaging wind, and a funnel cloud. Damage was most intense around Wamsutter where the combination of wind and hail obliterated vinyl siding and broke windows at numerous homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A remote weather station near Continental Divide on Interstate 80 at mile marker 184 recorded wind gusts of 65 to 68 mph for ten minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud reported to be just north of Wamsutter was photographed from Interstate 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting storm system off the Pacific continued to produce heavy snowfall into the early morning hours of April 1. Snowfall around one foot was estimated in the Salt River Range. Amounts in nearby Star Valley ranged from four to eight inches. This event began on March 31 (see March 2009 Storm Data).

A band of very heavy snow dropped amounts of two inches per hour across central Fremont County during the early morning of Thursday, April 9. The heaviest snow was localized to an area between Riverton and Pavillion where 8 to 12 inches accumulated. Most of the snow fell in a six-hour period between 0030 and 0630MST.

Widespread heavy snow fell across eastern portions of Fremont and Sweetwater counties in northeast upslope flow. The upslope developed in response to deepening low pressure in the Great Basin and was responsible for accumulations of five to nine inches across lower elevations. Higher totals of 12 to 16 inches were measured on the east slope of the Wind River Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow ahead of a Pacific low pressure system tracked a plume of moisture across northwest Wyoming. The result was 8 to 16 inches of snowfall over the higher terrain of central and southern Yellowstone National Park and the adjacent Absaroka Range to the east.  The heaviest snow fell in a swath from Grant Village to around Pahaska near the East Entrance to the park.

Persistent west-southwest flow of 35 to 45 mph brought moisture to the mountains of northwest Wyoming. Warm temperatures hindered snowfall accumulation. Still, storm totals of 8 to 14 inches occurred over the southern half of Yellowstone National Park. Some notable amounts were 14 inches at Two Ocean Plateau SNOTEL and 13 inches at Grant Village. Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph were frequent throughout the storm.

Strong westerly flow ahead of an approaching Pacific storm produced heavy snowfall in the mountains of far west Wyoming. Snowfall around Jackson Hole ranged from about 3 inches in Jackson to 13 inches at Moose. The nearby Teton Range and the higher terrain of Yellowstone National Park received 10 to 16 inches of snow. The exceptions were a few measuring sites at the Jackson Hole Mountain Resort which measured snowfall of 17 to 19 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second major snowstorm and blizzard within a weeks time brought heavy snow and strong winds to portions of Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. This storm impacted areas that were hit hard by the March 23-24 storm. An upper level low pressure moved out of the Pacific Northwest and moved across Idaho and Wyoming. At the same time, a strong surface low pressure developed over Southern Montana and Northern Wyoming. Winds across the area were sustained in the 25 to 35 mph range with gusts from 30 to 40 mph. These winds combined with heavy snow resulted in visibilities being reduced to a quarter mile at many locations. In addition, 50 mph wind gusts combined with around 18 inches of snow resulted in 8 foot drifts in the Sheridan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Domelake Snotel received 14 inches of snow while Big Goose Snotel received 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance on the west coast moved southward over the southern Rockies and then out onto the southern plains from approximately December 21st through the 26th. This system affected the Cheyenne CWA in two waves. The first occurred December 22 and 23rd and brought light snow with strong winds that created drifting snow and hazardous driving conditions. As the storm system moved eastward, it intensified and brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the central Plains over the Holiday weekend.  A second wave of significant winter weather occurred over southeast Wyoming and the western Nebraska panhandle in wraparound energy from late on the 24th through the 26th.  Heavy snow and very strong winds produced blizzard conditions in the northeast portions of the CWA with near blizzard conditions elsewhere. Many roads were closed, including interstates 80 and 25 on Christmas day. Two deaths were indirectly attributed to to the second wave of winter weather, one near Laramie, WY and the other near Guernsey, WY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were seen across the east central Wyoming through Christmas day and continued through the following morning. Area roads were closed due to snow and blowing snow reducing visibilities and creating hazardous roads.  Local law enforcement reported visibilities down to 100 yards or less for most of the morning hours due to very strong winds. The winds made it difficult to discern whether it was snowing or if it was just blowing snow, but was estimated that 3 inches of snow had fallen as of 25/1245 MST. At 25/1500 MST, law enforcement reported a continuation of low visibility less than 200 yards due to blowing snow.  At 25/2045 MST, the AWOS at Lusk reported continuing visibility less than 1/4 mile with heavy snow and northwest winds of 32 MPH gusting to 39 MPH.  Similar conditions were reported again at 26/0201 MST with sustained winds of 37 MPH gusting to 48 MPH.  Regarding snowfall, a public report came in from Van Tassell at 26/0900 with an estimated 4 inches of snow and 3 ft drifts.

Strongest winds were located in central and southern Laramie Range and the northern foothills of the Snowy Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Union Pacific Railroad sensor at Buford (UP203) sustained winds greater than 40 mph were reported, with a peak sustained wind of 52 mph at 15:50LST.  The peak gust of 56 mph occurred at 13:00LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass pushed into southwest Wyoming during the afternoon of Sunday, January 25 dropping temperatures 30 degrees over several hours. Northeast wind of 20 to 30 mph with occasional gusts of 35 to 40 mph created wind chill readings near 30 below zero early on January 26. Moisture from the southwest overran this cold dome and generated snowfall of four to eight inches across a good portion of Sweetwater County and into southern Natrona County. A separate upper level disturbance moved northeast across Lincoln County on Sunday evening. This disturbance quickly produced snowfall of six to eight inches in the Star Valley with 12 to 15 inches in the adjacent mountains.

Favorable northwest flow traveled down the Wind River Valley and produced strong wind in and around Riverton. An estimated gust of 71 mph tore a roof from a home that was under construction northwest of Riverton. Also, a large 50-year-old-plus spruce tree was toppled onto two vehicles parked in downtown Riverton. Both vehicles sustained damage. Other notable wind gusts of 62 mph at the NWS office and 59 mph at Riverton Regional Airport were recorded between 1430 and 1500MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice climber was killed when an avalanche one-quarter mile across swept 2,000 feet down a mountainside and spilled over the edge of a steep slope he was climbing. His climbing partner was uninjured. The two climbers experienced two smaller avalanches prior to the fatal slide during their more than 1,000 foot ascent. The slide occurred on The Main Vein, a popular climbing spot on the upper South Fork of the Shoshone River.
A strong pressure gradient produced high winds in wind favored areas of southeast Wyoming while heavy snow fell over the Snowy Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many other gusts from 60 to 70 mph recorded over much of central Laramie county including Cheyenne with a peak wind gust of 75 mph along Interstate 25 near the Whitaker interchange.  One truck driver was injured when his tractor-trailer trck was blown over along Interstate 25 near the Colorado border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance on the west coast moved southward over the southern Rockies and then out onto the southern plains from approximately December 21st through the 26th. This system affected the Cheyenne CWA in two waves. The first occurred December 22 and 23rd and brought light snow with strong winds that created drifting snow and hazardous driving conditions. As the storm system moved eastward, it intensified and brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the central Plains over the Holiday weekend.  A second wave of significant winter weather occurred over southeast Wyoming and the western Nebraska panhandle in wraparound energy from late on the 24th through the 26th.  Heavy snow and very strong winds produced blizzard conditions in the northeast portions of the CWA with near blizzard conditions elsewhere. Many roads were closed, including interstates 80 and 25 on Christmas day. Two deaths were indirectly attributed to to the second wave of winter weather, one near Laramie, WY and the other near Guernsey, WY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near blizzard conditions occurred over much of southeast Wyoming on Christmas day and continued through the late afternoon of the 26th. Area roads were closed due to snow and blowing snow reducing visibilities and creating hazardous driving conditions.  The ASOS at Cheyenne Airport reported a wind gust of 55 MPH at 25/1336 MST.  A Wyoming Department of Transportation site 4SSW of Cheyenne reported a gust of 58 MPH at 25/1340 MST. The combination of strong winds and snowfall created near blizzard conditions during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass pushed into southwest Wyoming during the afternoon of Sunday, January 25 dropping temperatures 30 degrees over several hours. Northeast wind of 20 to 30 mph with occasional gusts of 35 to 40 mph created wind chill readings near 30 below zero early on January 26. Moisture from the southwest overran this cold dome and generated snowfall of four to eight inches across a good portion of Sweetwater County and into southern Natrona County. A separate upper level disturbance moved northeast across Lincoln County on Sunday evening. This disturbance quickly produced snowfall of six to eight inches in the Star Valley with 12 to 15 inches in the adjacent mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, moist westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean provided a prolonged period of snowfall across northwest and far west Wyoming. Mountain snowfall generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches. The highest amounts were mainly confined to a swath from northeast Teton County, across southern Yellowstone National Park, and into the central Absaroka Range. For the lower elevations, the heaviest snow totals of four to eight inches occurred in the Bondurant Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to portions of northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the Wyoming Black Hills, where one to two feet of snow was reported. Gusty winds produced significant blowing and drifting of snow, reducing visibilities below a quarter mile at times.

A powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds to portions of northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the Wyoming Black Hills, where one to two feet of snow was reported. Gusty winds produced significant blowing and drifting of snow, reducing visibilities below a quarter mile at times.

Strong westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean brought abundant moisture to the mountains and valleys of western Wyoming.  Mountain snowfall generally ranged from around 16 to 23 inches. The highest totals of 20 to 23 inches fell in a swath from the northern Wyoming Range north through Teton County to the extreme south end of Yellowstone National Park. The Bondurant Valley and the north half of Star Valley recorded the most snowfall among western valley sites. Totals of 12 to 16 inches were common from Alpine to Thayne with a foot observed at Bondurant. Most other valley locations recorded totals of 6 to 10 inches.

A strong portion of the jet stream moved across Wyoming causing the pressure gradient at 700 mb and the surface to strengthen.  The tightening pressure gradient caused strong winds at 700 mb as well as the surface to develop.  Strong surface winds were reported across Carbon, Converse and Platte counties which lasted from the morning into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor at Arlington (KARL) measured sustained winds in excess of 40 MPH from approximately 0500 to 1000 LST, and again in the late afternoon hours from approximately 1530 through 1745 LST. Gusts of 59 MPH were measured at 1541 to 1546 LST.

Heavy, wet snow contributed to scattered power outages and numerous automobile accidents.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6-12 inches in the mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parkman received 6 inches of snow while 5NW of Dayton reported 12 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm moved across the Central Plains and Midwest and moved northwest into the Northern High Plains. Wraparound snow and strong winds backed into far Southeastern Montana. Heavy snowfall and strong winds developed on Christmas Day, Friday December 25th, and lasted into Saturday December 26th. Portions of Sheridan County, mainly the Big Horn Mountains, were impacted with snowfall of 12 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burgess Junction received between 12 and 16 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of four snowmobilers triggered a mile long, 100 yard wide avalanche in the Deadhorse Creek drainage near Alpine in Lincoln County. The group rode into a draw after dropping off a ridge line and the slab broke loose taking the lives of three men as they tried to outrun the slide. The lone survivor was able to get one hand above the snow and dig himself out. He then walked out and used a cell phone to call for help. The three victims were found more than two hours later under about three feet of snow.
A strong cold front produced northwest winds 30 to 45 mph with gusts around 55 mph.
A storm system brought snow to much of northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across northern Campbell County and the Wyoming Black Hills, where a total of five to eight inches were reported.
A storm system brought snow to much of northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across northern Campbell County and the Wyoming Black Hills, where a total of five to eight inches were reported.

A powerful winter storm brought blizzard conditions to northeastern Crook County. Snowfall totals over a foot and drifts five to ten feet high were reported. Visibility were near zero for over 24 hours.

Peak wind gusts over 70 mph were recorded in eastern sections of Fremont and Sweetwater counties as the result of a strong pressure gradient. Measured gusts included 74 mph at Camp Creek RAWS and 73 mph at Snow Springs RAWS. Spotters at the Buckboard Marina on the west shore of Flaming Gorge Reservoir estimated wind gusts up to 70 mph. The high wind toppled at least three power lines and ripped down a large commercial sign in Rock Springs. Problems with the downed power lines and a circuit breaker caused nearly 3,000 Rock Springs customers to lose power. In northern Wyoming, strong west-northwest flow produced sustained wind of 63 mph over Chief Joseph Pass.

Peak wind gusts over 70 mph were recorded in eastern sections of Fremont and Sweetwater counties as the result of a strong pressure gradient. Measured gusts included 74 mph at Camp Creek RAWS and 73 mph at Snow Springs RAWS. Spotters at the Buckboard Marina on the west shore of Flaming Gorge Reservoir estimated wind gusts up to 70 mph. The high wind toppled at least three power lines and ripped down a large commercial sign in Rock Springs. Problems with the downed power lines and a circuit breaker caused nearly 3,000 Rock Springs customers to lose power. In northern Wyoming, strong west-northwest flow produced sustained wind of 63 mph over Chief Joseph Pass.

A strong low pressure system deepened over eastern Colorado and produced widespread snow and strong winds across much of southeast Wyoming with blizzard or near blizzard conditions.  Many roads were closed due to significant blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts in excess of 50 mph and snow produced blizzard conditions at times.  Snowfall was estimated to be from 2 to 5 inches across Platte county with some drifting snow and very low visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm developed over eastern Colorado and produced snow and strong winds over far southeastern Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow combined with wind gusts around 45 mph to produce very low visibilities and some road closures due to blowing and drifting snow.  Snow was estimated to be from 4 to 5 inches in Cheyenne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong early season cold front brought snow to extreme southeast Wyoming and the southern Nebraska panhandle. Interstate 80 was closed between Cheyenne and Rawlins from the night of October 9 until midday on October 10. Interstate 25 south of Cheyenne was closed for periods as well as several county roads. Law enforcement agencies reported many car accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported over 8 inches of snow one mile southeast of Laramie.|Another report of 7 inches of snow in Laramie by law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A good surge of Pacific moisture combined with a vigorous cold front to produce widespread heavy snow in the mountains and nearby foothill areas of west-central Wyoming. Strong westerly flow allowed for snowfall of one to two feet in the Teton, Salt River, and Wyoming ranges of far west Wyoming. The nearby Jackson Hole measured new snowfall of 6 to 12 inches. East of the Continental Divide, the cold frontal push created ideal upslope conditions from the Wind River Range east to Casper. One to two feet of snow fell in the eastern Wind River Range with 6 to 12 inches commonly found from Lander to Jeffrey City to Casper. The heavy snow closed roads across Fremont County during the day on Thursday, March 26.

Low pressure emerged on the plains of Colorado and induced a strong pressure gradient across Wyoming. The result was a favorable pattern for upslope snow and associated wind of 25 to 40 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph. This combination resulted in blizzard or near-blizzard conditions. Snowfall across the lower elevations was mainly in the two to four inch range with isolated amounts to seven inches from Thermopolis to Riverton. The heavier band of snow necessitated the closure of State Highway 789 through the Wind River Canyon for over 12 hours. Snowfall from 7 to 13 inches was common in the Absaroka, Bighorn, and Wind River mountains. Johnson and Washakie counties experienced the worst of the snow and wind combination. Wind speeds in these counties were frequently 25 to 35 mph with gusts between 46 and 58 mph on Monday morning, March 30.

An arctic front moved across the Billings Forecast Area. The arctic air combined with a moist flow aloft brought a prolonged period of snow to Sheridan County. The heaviest snow fell in the mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas from Sheridan to Dayton received anywhere from 6 to 9 inches of snow.

West-northwest flow was accompanied by enough moisture to generate heavy orographic snow in the mountains of Yellowstone National Park and Teton County. The heaviest snow fell in a swath from the Teton Range to around the East Entrance of Yellowstone National Park. Snowfall totals reached 10 to 16 inches within this swath.

Low pressure in Colorado produced a prolonged period of northeast upslope flow across central and southern Wyoming. The result was four to eight inch snowfall accumulations across the lower elevations. Hardest hit areas included Johnson, Natrona, Fremont, and Sweetwater counties. Mountain snowfall was mainly confined to the 10 to 18 inch range. The exception was the east slope of the Wind River Mountains where accumulations up to two feet were estimated over the southern portion of the range. State Highway 28 over South Pass was closed as northerly wind gusted between 40 and 50 mph. A peak gust of 55 mph was measured on the pass shortly after 0200MST on Saturday, April 4. The combination of snowfall and wind closed many roads heading east or south from Natrona, Fremont, and Sweetwater counties.

A quick-hitting storm system off the Pacific brought heavy snow to the mountains and valleys of Lincoln County. Snowfall began during the early morning on Tuesday, March 31. Snow continued to fall through the day and into the early morning hours of April 1 (see April 2009 Storm Data for more information).

A severe thunderstorm traveled along Interstate 80 for about 40 miles through eastern Sweetwater County. The storm produced large hail, damaging wind, and a funnel cloud. Damage was most intense around Wamsutter where the combination of wind and hail obliterated vinyl siding and broke windows at numerous homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell along Interstate 80 from nine miles west of Wamsutter to Wamsutter.

A powerful spring storm brought blizzard conditions to northeastern Wyoming. Heavy snow and wind gusts over 60 mph produced significant blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities to near zero for many hours. Snowfall of one to two feet were common across the area, with the highest amounts over the Bear Lodge Mountains and Wyoming Black Hills. Interstate 90 and many roads were closed for more than 24 hours. Some power outages were also reported. Many livestock perished.

The second blizzard in a week brought heavy snow and strong winds to northeastern Wyoming, which produced near zero visibilities and blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall amounts were generally six to 12 inches, with locally higher amounts, and drifts several feet high. Many highways, including Interstate 90, were closed for many hours.

Snowfall amounts were six to 12 inches, with higher amounts over the Wyoming Black Hills. Gusty winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow, with visibilities below a quarter of a mile. Many area roads were closed.

Many area roads were closed.
A strong cold front and upper level system brought heavy snow to portions of western Crook County. As much as eight inches of snow was measured in the Hulett area.

A cold front slid south along the east slopes of the Continental Divide and dropped temperatures into the single digits and teens. Moist southwest flow rode over this cold dome of air and helped to produce widespread snowfall amounts of four to eight inches across the central basins. The heaviest snow fell in the eastern Wind River Mountains where snow totals ranged from 10 to 16 inches. Hardest hit lower elevation sites were Lander, Riverton, Kaycee, Buffalo, and Midwest where snowfall of six to eight inches was reported. Brisk northeast wind of 15 to 25 mph caused local white-out conditions and made for icy travel across the central corridor of the state.

West-northwest flow was accompanied by enough moisture to generate heavy orographic snow in the mountains of Yellowstone National Park and Teton County. The heaviest snow fell in a swath from the Teton Range to around the East Entrance of Yellowstone National Park. Snowfall totals reached 10 to 16 inches within this swath.

The heaviest snow fell in a swath from the Teton Range to around the East Entrance of Yellowstone National Park. Snowfall totals reached 10 to 16 inches within this swath.

Low pressure strengthened over the plains of Colorado producing upslope flow across central Wyoming east of the Continental Divide. The storm produced gusty northerly wind and created blizzard conditions across Johnson County. Wind gusts of 40 mph or more were recorded for approximately 25 hours at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo. Speeds at the airport topped 60 mph early in the morning on Tuesday, March 24 with a peak of 68 mph shortly after 0100MST. The combination of snow and wind shut down state and federal highways in Johnson and Natrona counties. Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph were also routine in Big Horn and Washakie counties on Monday, March 23. Sustained wind of 40 mph with a peak gust to 68 mph was recorded in the foothills of the Owl Creek Mountains in Fremont County early Monday.||The heaviest snow fell in Natrona County where amounts of 8 to 16 inches combined with the wind to shut down schools, businesses, and government offices. On Casper Mountain, snowfall was estimated between two and three feet. Generally, snowfall totals across the lower elevations ranged from four to eight inches, while totals ranged from 12 to 18 inches in the mountains.  Most areas reported treacherous, if not impossible, travel conditions due to the very gusty wind reducing visibility.

A severe thunderstorm traveled along Interstate 80 for about 40 miles through eastern Sweetwater County. The storm produced large hail, damaging wind, and a funnel cloud. Damage was most intense around Wamsutter where the combination of wind and hail obliterated vinyl siding and broke windows at numerous homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed and at least one home under construction was damaged in Wamsutter by strong thunderstorm wind.

A good surge of Pacific moisture combined with a vigorous cold front to produce widespread heavy snow in the mountains and nearby foothill areas of west-central Wyoming. Strong westerly flow allowed for snowfall of one to two feet in the Teton, Salt River, and Wyoming ranges of far west Wyoming. The nearby Jackson Hole measured new snowfall of 6 to 12 inches. East of the Continental Divide, the cold frontal push created ideal upslope conditions from the Wind River Range east to Casper. One to two feet of snow fell in the eastern Wind River Range with 6 to 12 inches commonly found from Lander to Jeffrey City to Casper. The heavy snow closed roads across Fremont County during the day on Thursday, March 26.

The same moist winter storm that brought heavy snow to the higher terrain of Utah also brought heavy snow to southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mountain View received 12 inches of new snow for the event.

A lightning strike occurred about 20 miles west of Casper igniting a fire at an oil field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike destroyed or damaged five oil tanks about 20 miles west of Casper near the intersection of county roads 201 and 210. The strike occurred about 1300MST blowing the lid off one tank containing about 400 barrels of crude oil. The fire quickly spread to a nearby tank and burning crude oil ignited the other tanks. Two additional propane tanks were burned but did not explode. A nearly century old storage building at the site was also completely destroyed.

Scattered severe thunderstorms produced some hail and flooding across parts of southeast Wyoming.

Breaks in an overcast sky caused by moist upslope flow allowed for afternoon heating in the Wind River Basin. Thunderstorms developed near Ft. Washakie and tracked southeast along the foothills through Lander and Red Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Red Canyon Ranch experienced golf ball sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving down the Wind River Valley produced strong winds northwest of Morton and near Riverton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Riverton Regional Airport ASOS recorded a thunderstorm wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moving down the Wind River Valley produced strong winds northwest of Morton and near Riverton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gusts of 57 to 61 mph lifted a 16x40 foot metal structure which was staked to the ground and tossed it 60 to 70 feet downwind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breaks in an overcast sky caused by moist upslope flow allowed for afternoon heating in the Wind River Basin. Thunderstorms developed near Ft. Washakie and tracked southeast along the foothills through Lander and Red Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported on Trout Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon allowed sufficient heating to generate severe thunderstorms across western Wyoming. Instability was enhanced in moist southwest flow ahead of slow moving low pressure in California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of nickels was reported at Signal Mountain Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon allowed sufficient heating to generate severe thunderstorms across western Wyoming. Instability was enhanced in moist southwest flow ahead of slow moving low pressure in California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated sensor on Chief Joseph Pass measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon allowed sufficient heating to generate severe thunderstorms across western Wyoming. Instability was enhanced in moist southwest flow ahead of slow moving low pressure in California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds topped out at 61 mph in Ralston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon allowed sufficient heating to generate severe thunderstorms across western Wyoming. Instability was enhanced in moist southwest flow ahead of slow moving low pressure in California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters estimated wind gusts of at least 60 mph near Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon allowed sufficient heating to generate severe thunderstorms across western Wyoming. Instability was enhanced in moist southwest flow ahead of slow moving low pressure in California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated weather station on the far west side of Powell recorded a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 58.3 mph at 1708MST. The wind dropped a tree on North Douglas Street in Powell and stopped traffic for 30 minutes. Seven other toppled trees in an older section of town blocked streets for approximately three hours until city crews could clear the debris using chainsaws.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon allowed sufficient heating to generate severe thunderstorms across western Wyoming. Instability was enhanced in moist southwest flow ahead of slow moving low pressure in California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind of 60 mph was measured on ASOS at Rock Springs-Sweetwater County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the afternoon allowed sufficient heating to generate severe thunderstorms across western Wyoming. Instability was enhanced in moist southwest flow ahead of slow moving low pressure in California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail and wind gusts approaching 50 mph were reported at the Signal Mountain Marina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Absaroka Mountains, moved east into the Big Horn Basin, and produced damaging wind and large hail. The strongest storm traveled from just north of Meeteetse to Greybull to Shell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed one large tree which blocked a road near the Greybull library. Also, newspaper stands were blown over in Greybull.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow, north moving thunderstorm spawned a funnel cloud east of Casper which was seen by a large number of people.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of a funnel cloud east of Casper were received and the funnel was widely photographed. The funnel lasted for upwards of 20 minutes and tracked into Converse County (see Storm Data Wyoming, Southeast for information on the final segment of this funnel cloud). Over 90 calls were received at Natrona County dispatch regarding the funnel cloud. Emergency management followed the funnel and parent thunderstorm to the Converse County line on Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms crested the Teton Range and produced wind gusts strong enough to down several trees and power lines in Teton County, including Grand Teton National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed around Jenny Lake and across Grand Teton National Park. One vehicle on the Jenny Lake Scenic Loop lost its windshield because of a fallen tree. Most trees were less than one foot in diameter. A mesonet station about four miles south recorded a peak wind gust of 54 mph from the west-northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that developed northwest of Dubois traveled along the Wind River Valley and walloped central Fremont County with hail and heavy rain. Hail up to several inches deep accumulated on U.S. Highway 26 northwest of Riverton near Kinnear. The worst of the flash flooding was in a swath from Riverton east along the Gas Hills Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of one-half inch to one inch in diameter fell and accumulated northwest of Dubois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that developed northwest of Dubois traveled along the Wind River Valley and walloped central Fremont County with hail and heavy rain. Hail up to several inches deep accumulated on U.S. Highway 26 northwest of Riverton near Kinnear. The worst of the flash flooding was in a swath from Riverton east along the Gas Hills Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall estimated at 1 to 2.5 inches fell in about 30 minutes along Gas Hills Road. WYDOT engineers reported 500 feet of shoulder was eroded from the road due to running water near mile marker 6.5. Two culverts in the area could not handle the extreme volume of water. Approximately three to four feet of the shoulder and road surface was swept away leaving a vertical drop of 12 to 14 feet. Witnesses said three feet of water was above the road surface along a quarter-mile stretch of the road for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone cluster of thunderstorms was responsible for straight-line wind damage across eastern portions of the Big Horn Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several three-foot diameter Cottonwood trees were toppled at Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site northeast of Hyattville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in a dry environment setting the stage for microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst winds of 50 to 60 mph uprooted a 50-foot Cottonwood tree and blew a one-foot diameter branch about 50 feet in Hot Springs State Park. The wind was also responsible for moving a metal shed across a street near the Hot Springs County fairgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope flow behind a cold front combined with a favorable wind environment helped to generate strong to severe thunderstorms in Fremont and Johnson counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of nickels covered portions of U.S. Highway 16 and Interstate 25 to a depth of three to four inches. The combination of rain and hail caused one rancher to lose his hay crop on Upper French Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low drifted slowly southeast across western Wyoming during the afternoon hours. Cold air aloft coupled with daytime heating helped to generate a few severe thunderstorms in Fremont and Hot Springs counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts measured as high as 74 mph caused damage to more than a dozen mature trees at the Riverton Country Club. A 40-foot spruce tree was toppled and several limbs up to nine inches in diameter were brought down during the storm. Rainfall of one-half inch to upwards of one inch came down in sheets. The storm generally followed Riverview Road on the south side of U.S. Highway 26. One rancher claimed it was the hardest he had seen it rain in his 35 years at his location near Kinnear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms crested the Teton Range and produced wind gusts strong enough to down several trees and power lines in Teton County, including Grand Teton National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in a swath from the Death Canyon Trailhead east across the Moose-Wilson Road. Motorists were trapped on the road by fallen trees which were blocking passage. One vehicle was damaged near Death Canyon due to a fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense rainfall accompanied a strong thunderstorm which gained strength just west of Casper before blasting through town.  The thunderstorm formed in a uncharacteristically moist airmass where precipitable water values approached 150% of normal.  Longtime residents reported this to be the worst flash flooding they had seen in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph were estimated near the Wyoming Medical Center in association with a thunderstorm that produced widespread flash flooding in Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Developing thunderstorms west of Barnum spawned a brief funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Members of a Washakie County Road and Bridge crew observed a funnel cloud over the Big Horn Mountains west of Barnum. The crew was working near Cherry Creek Road in southeast Washakie County and observed the storm from the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed in weak upslope flow and produced some hail over parts of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accumulated up to 2.5 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed in weak upslope flow and produced some hail over parts of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported from 3/4 to 1 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in a moist, upslope flow airmass east of the Continental Divide. The severity of the storms was aided by a disturbance that moved northeast across the area and a favorable jet stream position.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were estimated at 55 to 65 mph along State Highway 135 near Sand Draw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in a moist, upslope flow airmass east of the Continental Divide. The severity of the storms was aided by a disturbance that moved northeast across the area and a favorable jet stream position.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one to two inches fell in and around Casper. One location at Wyoming Medical Center recorded 1.75 inches of rain. The water accumulated at the intersection of Poplar and CY avenues and flooded the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm formed just east of the Absaroka foothills and rumbled through northern Hot Springs County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts of 60 mph broke several three inch diameter limbs from Cottonwood trees at a ranch along Gooseberry Creek. Also, dime sized hail accumulated to a depth of two to three inches at the ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that developed northwest of Dubois traveled along the Wind River Valley and walloped central Fremont County with hail and heavy rain. Hail up to several inches deep accumulated on U.S. Highway 26 northwest of Riverton near Kinnear. The worst of the flash flooding was in a swath from Riverton east along the Gas Hills Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail accumulated to a depth of three or four inches along U.S. Highway 26 and nearby areas. The combination of rain and accumulating hail destroyed at least 200 tons of hay in fields around Kinnear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A non-supercell tornado struck a ranch about nine miles east of Manderson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado struck a ranch, including a home, east of Manderson. Three children were in the house at the time of the tornado. The tornado was about 100 feet wide and traveled approximately one-third mile from north-to-south, crossing State Highway 31 before dissipating. Several trees, two fences, and a wooden corral sustained considerable damage. An All-Terrain Vehicle (ATV) was lifted, spun 180 degrees, and moved about 15 feet. A bicycle was tossed onto the roof of the ranch home and several water bins were tossed about.||This landspout tornado occurred in a post-cold frontal environment. A cold front moved through Manderson around 0830MST on Monday leaving northwest wind of 30 to 40 mph in its wake. Solar heating continued to warm the surface while colder air streamed into the region aloft. This helped set the stage for an unstable atmosphere during the afternoon.  A west-moving boundary from showers to the east of Manderson in the Bighorn Mountain foothills helped initiate the updraft that spawned the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Absaroka Mountains, moved east into the Big Horn Basin, and produced damaging wind and large hail. The strongest storm traveled from just north of Meeteetse to Greybull to Shell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters fell along Road 3LD north of Meeteetse. This storm damaged some dry bean fields and forced one farmer to replant the crop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Absaroka Mountains, moved east into the Big Horn Basin, and produced damaging wind and large hail. The strongest storm traveled from just north of Meeteetse to Greybull to Shell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 55 knots was recorded at South Big Horn County Airport north of Greybull.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Absaroka Mountains, moved east into the Big Horn Basin, and produced damaging wind and large hail. The strongest storm traveled from just north of Meeteetse to Greybull to Shell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter along U.S. Highway 14 reported estimated winds around 70 mph and numerous branches blown off trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Absaroka Mountains, moved east into the Big Horn Basin, and produced damaging wind and large hail. The strongest storm traveled from just north of Meeteetse to Greybull to Shell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Branches were blown down in estimated 60 mph winds east of Greybull.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Absaroka Mountains, moved east into the Big Horn Basin, and produced damaging wind and large hail. The strongest storm traveled from just north of Meeteetse to Greybull to Shell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down near Beaver Creek west of Shell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense rainfall accompanied a strong thunderstorm which gained strength just west of Casper before blasting through town.  The thunderstorm formed in a uncharacteristically moist airmass where precipitable water values approached 150% of normal.  Longtime residents reported this to be the worst flash flooding they had seen in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vicious thunderstorm struck the city of Casper between about 1715 and 1800MST at the start of the July 4th holiday weekend. Extreme rainfall rates falling on the urbanized landscape produced flash flooding throughout Casper, with the most extensive inundation centered around the intersection of Poplar and Collins streets.||Nearly one inch of rain fell in 21 minutes at the Natrona County International Airport northwest of the city. A precipitation gauge along the North Platte River in Casper reported 1.79 inches of rain in 30 minutes. Final tallies around town showed rainfall from around one inch up to a high of about 2.25 inches occurred within about a 35 minute time frame.||Several streets, including Poplar and McKinley were reported to be rivers carrying rocks and other debris toward downtown Casper. Reports indicated one to three feet of water was present on some city streets. The flash flooding floated several cars and sent storm drains shooting wildly into the air. One storm drain cover injured an individual as the surging water displaced it. Emergency management estimated anywhere from 800 to 1000 structures, mainly homes, were impacted by the flooding.||In addition to hundreds of impacted homes, many well known locations also sustained damage, including the Nicolaysen Art Museum, Wyoming Medical Center, Central Wyoming Fairgrounds, Three Crowns Golf Course, and several city school buildings. Major damage also occurred at Casper College where five buildings, including the gymnasium and theater, were significantly impacted. Retaining wall bricks and landscape gravel were washed from a hillside at the college and cascaded to neighboring residences and streets below. The Hall of Justice on North David Street also sustained significant damage when water poured through a door into an underground garage. The building's elevator shafts and the ceilings in several sheriff's offices were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that developed northwest of Dubois traveled along the Wind River Valley and walloped central Fremont County with hail and heavy rain. Hail up to several inches deep accumulated on U.S. Highway 26 northwest of Riverton near Kinnear. The worst of the flash flooding was in a swath from Riverton east along the Gas Hills Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured rainfall in Riverton ranged from 0.99 inches to 1.58 inches in a span of 20 to 40 minutes. Storm drains could not keep up with the intense rain which produced two to three feet of standing water at the intersection of Federal Boulevard and Monroe Avenue and nearby parking lots. Other impacted streets in Riverton included North 8th West, East Pershing, and North 16th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced flash flooding northwest of Thermopolis along with a swath of quarter sized hail that stretched into town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a severe thunderstorm produced widespread flash flooding northwest of Thermopolis. Radar estimated 30 to 40 minute rainfall amounts to be from 1.00 to 1.75 inches. The flash flooding was primarily concentrated along state highways 120 and 170 near Owl Creek. Areas hardest hit along highway 120 extended from mile markers five to nine. Six homes were evacuated in this four mile stretch and at least two of the homes were flooded. One resident estimated water depth to be three to six feet, which damaged outbuildings, a well house, and a cistern. Some hay fields were reported to be waist deep in water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened over the plains of Colorado producing upslope flow across central Wyoming east of the Continental Divide. The storm produced gusty northerly wind and created blizzard conditions across Johnson County. Wind gusts of 40 mph or more were recorded for approximately 25 hours at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo. Speeds at the airport topped 60 mph early in the morning on Tuesday, March 24 with a peak of 68 mph shortly after 0100MST. The combination of snow and wind shut down state and federal highways in Johnson and Natrona counties. Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph were also routine in Big Horn and Washakie counties on Monday, March 23. Sustained wind of 40 mph with a peak gust to 68 mph was recorded in the foothills of the Owl Creek Mountains in Fremont County early Monday.||The heaviest snow fell in Natrona County where amounts of 8 to 16 inches combined with the wind to shut down schools, businesses, and government offices. On Casper Mountain, snowfall was estimated between two and three feet. Generally, snowfall totals across the lower elevations ranged from four to eight inches, while totals ranged from 12 to 18 inches in the mountains.  Most areas reported treacherous, if not impossible, travel conditions due to the very gusty wind reducing visibility.

Low pressure emerged on the plains of Colorado and induced a strong pressure gradient across Wyoming. The result was a favorable pattern for upslope snow and associated wind of 25 to 40 mph with gusts in excess of 50 mph. This combination resulted in blizzard or near-blizzard conditions. Snowfall across the lower elevations was mainly in the two to four inch range with isolated amounts to seven inches from Thermopolis to Riverton. The heavier band of snow necessitated the closure of State Highway 789 through the Wind River Canyon for over 12 hours. Snowfall from 7 to 13 inches was common in the Absaroka, Bighorn, and Wind River mountains. Johnson and Washakie counties experienced the worst of the snow and wind combination. Wind speeds in these counties were frequently 25 to 35 mph with gusts between 46 and 58 mph on Monday morning, March 30.

The largest severe weather outbreak of the summer occurred during the afternoon and evening hours of the 7th. A moist and very unstable atmosphere was in place across South Central and Southeast Montana, as well as Northern Wyoming.  An upper level disturbance combined with a cool front moving across the Billings County Warning Area allowed for the development of severe thunderstorms producing high winds and large hail. Very heavy rain also accompanied the thunderstorms. The bulk of the severe weather occurred across Southern and Southeast Montana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited the Arnica wildfire on Sunday, September 13 in old growth, Lodgepole timber west of Bridge Bay in Yellowstone National Park. The fire was not detected until September 23 when it was discovered to be four acres in size. Warm, dry, and windy conditions allowed the fire to grow to about 1,600 acres over the following two days. A ridge of high pressure and unseasonably warm temperatures on September 26 allowed the fire to expand to an estimated 8,300 acres in size. Additional growth on September 27 of 1,000 acres enabled the fire to burn within 3.5 miles of Lake Village. Burnt trees were brought to the ground on the Grand Loop Road between Fishing Bridge and West Thumb during the afternoon as fire swept across the road.  At its peak, the fire burned actively along a 1.5 mile stretch of the road.||Over 200 people and five helicopters actively fought the fire and protected nearby structures. Brush was cleared from around structures and hose lays and sprinkler systems were set-up at Bridge Bay and Lake Village. A change in the weather pattern brought rain, snow, and colder temperatures to the fire on September 30. Personnel were quickly demobilized and sprinklers were removed from structures. All told, the fire charred approximately 10,700 acres of land.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Campbell County and moved southeastward across Crook County. The storm produced very large hail across western Crook County, which caused significant damage in the New Haven and Hulett areas. The storm also spawned a large tornado over the Bear Lodge Mountains north of Sundance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifty mph wind gusts were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tornado touched down west of Cook Lake and tracked east-southeastward across the Bear Lodge Mountains, dissipating west of Wyoming Highway 111. The tornado downed about 4000 acres of Ponderosa pine trees, destroyed two metal shop buildings and a large barn at a ranch, and severely damaged a house and mobile home. Two people in the residences received very minor injuries.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL LARAMIE RANGE AND SOUTHWEST PLATTE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system moved across the area creating a large pressure gradient that produce a couple of hours of high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wyoming Department of Transportation sensor (KBDX) recorded sustained winds in excess of 40 mph between 10 AM and Noon with a peak gust of 67 mph at 1120 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large trough over Western Wyoming combined with a surface low across Colorado resulted in a major early Fall snowstorm across North Central Wyoming. Heavy, wet snow contributed to scattered power outages and numerous automobile accidents.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6-12 inches in the mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tie Creek Snotel and 5NW of Dayton each reported 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system moved across the area creating a large pressure gradient that produce a couple of hours of high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A UP railroad sensor at Lynch recorded sustained winds in excess of 40 mph between 10 and 11 AM with a peak gust of 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean brought abundant moisture to the mountains and valleys of western Wyoming.  Mountain snowfall generally ranged from around 16 to 23 inches. The highest totals of 20 to 23 inches fell in a swath from the northern Wyoming Range north through Teton County to the extreme south end of Yellowstone National Park. The Bondurant Valley and the north half of Star Valley recorded the most snowfall among western valley sites. Totals of 12 to 16 inches were common from Alpine to Thayne with a foot observed at Bondurant. Most other valley locations recorded totals of 6 to 10 inches.

A tightening surface pressure gradient and unseasonably warm temperatures allowed for strong west-southwest wind to mix to the surface. The wind was strongest along U.S. Highway 287 over Beaver Rim where a peak wind of 61 mph was measured. Sustained wind over 40 mph frequently occurred during the very early afternoon hours.

A deep low pressure system carved a path from the Four Corners region northeast onto the plains of Colorado, Nebraska, and Kansas on Wednesday and Thursday, October 28 and 29. Snow and gusty wind wrapped around the northwest side of the low pressure impacting central and parts of southwest Wyoming. The combination of wind and snow reduced visibility, iced roads, and made for difficult travel at times. Hardest hit were areas from around South Pass east to Casper Mountain.||The southeast end of the Wind River Mountains near South Pass had estimated snowfall of 18 to 29 inches during the storm. Similar amounts were noted on Casper Mountain where drifts up to four feet high were reported. Snowfall amounts from Lander to Jeffrey City to Casper generally ranged from four to eight inches. While lesser amounts were observed in eastern Sweetwater County, northeast wind of 30 mph to around 50 mph persisted through much of the day on Wednesday. No unnecessary travel was advised by Wyoming DOT on several area roads. However, the only road closure occurred on State Highway 28 over South Pass on Wednesday morning. A mesonet station on the pass recorded visibility as low as 1/8 mile as the northerly wind routinely blew at 40 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. The peak speed of 63 mph was recorded by this sensor shortly after noon Wednesday.

A strong Pacific storm brought cold air and abundant moisture to the area. A long duration upslope flow created favorable conditions for heavy snow. This storm produced dangerous winter storm conditions over a wide area of southeast Wyoming and western Nebraska. Interstates 80 and 25, as well as many state and local highways, were closed for significant periods of time causing problems for travelers and commerce. Reports of over 100 accidents were reported. One fatality was reported. There were many reports of snowfall in excess of 20 inches. As the snowfall was winding down, strong northerly winds were observed creating additional issues with blowing and drifting snow. Reports came in of drifts in the 4 to 6 feet range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 12 inches of snow in Pine Bluffs at 8 AM on 10/29/2009 along with blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Great Basin brought copious moisture and temperatures just cold enough to produce heavy snow to parts of central and northern Wyoming. An easterly low-level wind flow enabled the heaviest snow to fall in upslope favored mountains and valleys from Sublette County, to Fremont County, and north into Park County. Moisture wrapping around the system on Sunday night and Monday allowed for heavy snow to also accumulate in northern Johnson County.||Snowfall totals ranged from 7 to 14 inches across many lower elevation sites in Fremont County. The highest totals of 12 to 14 inches were found in Lander and Jeffrey City. The nearby east slopes of the Wind River Mountains received one to nearly two feet of snow. Amounts just topping one foot fell in the Absaroka Range. Final snow tallies around Big Piney-Marbleton in western Sublette County and near Buffalo in Johnson County topped out between six and eight inches. Some localized amounts of four to eight inches were reported between Meeteetse and Cody.||Recent warm temperatures enabled travel to remain relatively unimpeded as snow melted quickly off area highways and streets. The worst problems were caused by falling tree limbs, mainly in the Wind River Basin and around Buffalo. Trees in these areas still had green leaves on them when the storm struck. The weight of the heavy snow caused limbs nearly six inches in diameter to snap. In Buffalo, most of the town was left without power as thousands of limbs were brought to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Great Basin brought copious moisture and temperatures just cold enough to produce heavy snow to parts of central and northern Wyoming. An easterly low-level wind flow enabled the heaviest snow to fall in upslope favored mountains and valleys from Sublette County, to Fremont County, and north into Park County. Moisture wrapping around the system on Sunday night and Monday allowed for heavy snow to also accumulate in northern Johnson County.||Snowfall totals ranged from 7 to 14 inches across many lower elevation sites in Fremont County. The highest totals of 12 to 14 inches were found in Lander and Jeffrey City. The nearby east slopes of the Wind River Mountains received one to nearly two feet of snow. Amounts just topping one foot fell in the Absaroka Range. Final snow tallies around Big Piney-Marbleton in western Sublette County and near Buffalo in Johnson County topped out between six and eight inches. Some localized amounts of four to eight inches were reported between Meeteetse and Cody.||Recent warm temperatures enabled travel to remain relatively unimpeded as snow melted quickly off area highways and streets. The worst problems were caused by falling tree limbs, mainly in the Wind River Basin and around Buffalo. Trees in these areas still had green leaves on them when the storm struck. The weight of the heavy snow caused limbs nearly six inches in diameter to snap. In Buffalo, most of the town was left without power as thousands of limbs were brought to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system carved a path from the Four Corners region northeast onto the plains of Colorado, Nebraska, and Kansas on Wednesday and Thursday, October 28 and 29. Snow and gusty wind wrapped around the northwest side of the low pressure impacting central and parts of southwest Wyoming. The combination of wind and snow reduced visibility, iced roads, and made for difficult travel at times. Hardest hit were areas from around South Pass east to Casper Mountain.||The southeast end of the Wind River Mountains near South Pass had estimated snowfall of 18 to 29 inches during the storm. Similar amounts were noted on Casper Mountain where drifts up to four feet high were reported. Snowfall amounts from Lander to Jeffrey City to Casper generally ranged from four to eight inches. While lesser amounts were observed in eastern Sweetwater County, northeast wind of 30 mph to around 50 mph persisted through much of the day on Wednesday. No unnecessary travel was advised by Wyoming DOT on several area roads. However, the only road closure occurred on State Highway 28 over South Pass on Wednesday morning. A mesonet station on the pass recorded visibility as low as 1/8 mile as the northerly wind routinely blew at 40 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. The peak speed of 63 mph was recorded by this sensor shortly after noon Wednesday.

Interstates 80 and 25, as well as many state and local highways, were closed for significant periods of time causing problems for travelers and commerce. Reports of over 100 accidents were reported. One fatality was reported. There were many reports of snowfall in excess of 20 inches. As the snowfall was winding down, strong northerly winds were observed creating additional issues with blowing and drifting snow. Reports came in of drifts in the 4 to 6 feet range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer located 7 miles northeast of Cheyenne reported 11 inches of new snow at 7 AM on 10/29/2009 with a storm snowfall total of 20 inches. Snow water equivalent of 1.16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Great Basin brought copious moisture and temperatures just cold enough to produce heavy snow to parts of central and northern Wyoming. An easterly low-level wind flow enabled the heaviest snow to fall in upslope favored mountains and valleys from Sublette County, to Fremont County, and north into Park County. Moisture wrapping around the system on Sunday night and Monday allowed for heavy snow to also accumulate in northern Johnson County.||Snowfall totals ranged from 7 to 14 inches across many lower elevation sites in Fremont County. The highest totals of 12 to 14 inches were found in Lander and Jeffrey City. The nearby east slopes of the Wind River Mountains received one to nearly two feet of snow. Amounts just topping one foot fell in the Absaroka Range. Final snow tallies around Big Piney-Marbleton in western Sublette County and near Buffalo in Johnson County topped out between six and eight inches. Some localized amounts of four to eight inches were reported between Meeteetse and Cody.||Recent warm temperatures enabled travel to remain relatively unimpeded as snow melted quickly off area highways and streets. The worst problems were caused by falling tree limbs, mainly in the Wind River Basin and around Buffalo. Trees in these areas still had green leaves on them when the storm struck. The weight of the heavy snow caused limbs nearly six inches in diameter to snap. In Buffalo, most of the town was left without power as thousands of limbs were brought to the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail from 3/4 to 1 inch in diameter reported over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms crested the Teton Range and produced wind gusts strong enough to down several trees and power lines in Teton County, including Grand Teton National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down in Wilson as was a six inch diameter tree just west of town. A local energy company had to install one new pole and rebuild six others. One power line fell on Fish Creek Road and stopped traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable air in place over Johnson and Natrona counties triggered afternoon and evening thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of penny size fell near the foothills of the Bighorn Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm that developed northwest of Dubois traveled along the Wind River Valley and walloped central Fremont County with hail and heavy rain. Hail up to several inches deep accumulated on U.S. Highway 26 northwest of Riverton near Kinnear. The worst of the flash flooding was in a swath from Riverton east along the Gas Hills Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail fell in a subdivision northwest of Riverton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late night severe thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Wyoming, producing golf ball sized hail and strong wind gusts.
Southwest flow ahead of an upper low over the Intermountain West and a favorably unstable atmosphere combined to generate hail and an isolated tornado over the southern Big Horn Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-supercell tornado was photographed from Worland over southern Washakie County. The tornado was short-lived and caused no damage over open range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused some flooding along Cottonwood Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall flooded many roads and made them impassable for a time.
